sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, MOUSE(row=50, col=30).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 26, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking on squares in the right panel changes their color! The white square at top-middle turned green (N). The handle state likely determines what color gets applied. Need to map handle states to colors and understand the goal (likely matching the left panel's pattern).
- Plan: Explore the color mapping by clicking more squares with different handle states. First, let me reset the changed square and test systematically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 25 of about 36 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so after moving DOWN and clicking on the top-middle square:
- The top-middle square turned green (N) - same paint color as before
- The handle changed: the right part now has S on top and b on bottom (vertical), whereas before it was S on left and b on right (horizontal)
So moving the handle DOWN changed its orientation from horizontal to vertical. The paint color is still green.
Let me track the handle states more carefully:
- Initial state: S 2x2 at left, b small segment at right (handle at far left)
- After moving right: S and b become horizontal segments at right end
- After moving down: S and b become vertical segments at right end
The handle orientation seems to change based on the last directional key pressed. But the paint color is always green.
I'm running out of actions (25 of 36). Let me try a different approach.
Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. Clicking on the gray squares in the right panel (I did this earlier and they turned green)
2. Clicking on the handle itself
3. Clicking on the green bar on the right edge
4. Moving the handle to specific positions and then clicking on specific squares
5. Using the handle's position to select which square to paint
Let me try clicking on the green bar on the right edge to see if it changes the paint color.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the left panel's pattern. The left panel has:
- Dark gray (G) outer frame
- Gray (g) fill forming a cross/diamond
- Purple (p) dots at 4 tips
The right panel has:
- Dark gray (G) outer frame
- A 3x3 grid
- White squares at 4 cross positions (matching purple dots)
- Gray squares at corners and center
What if the goal is to make the right panel look EXACTLY like the left panel? The left panel has:
- Gray (g) fill in a cross/diamond shape
- Purple (p) dots at 4 tips
- Dark gray (G) corners
So the right panel should have:
- Gray fill in cross/diamond shape (the gray squares are already gray)
- Purple dots at 4 tips (the white squares should become purple)
- Dark gray corners (the corner squares are already dark gray)
But the paint color is green, not purple. Unless I can change the paint color somehow.
Wait, maybe the handle's colors (S and b) are the paint colors, not the green bar. Let me think about this:
- The handle has sky blue (S) and dark blue (b)
- Maybe one handle color paints one way and the other paints another way
- The handle orientation (horizontal vs vertical) selects which color is active
Let me test: when the handle is horizontal (S left, b right), maybe S is the active color. When vertical (S top, b bottom), maybe b is the active color.
But I've been painting green (N) regardless of handle orientation. So this doesn't seem right.
Unless the paint color is determined by something else. Let me look at the green bar more carefully. It's a vertical green bar on the right edge. Maybe this bar is the "paint color selector" and I need to click on it to change the paint color.
Actually, let me try something completely different. Let me look at the INITIAL state of the game (before any actions) and see if there's a pattern I'm missing.
The initial state had:
- Left panel: cross/diamond with purple dots
- Right panel: 3x3 grid with white squares at cross positions
- Handle: at far left with S 2x2 square
- Green bar: on right edge
Maybe the goal is to move the handle to match the left panel's pattern. The left panel has 4 purple dots. Maybe the handle needs to be moved to 4 different positions, one for each dot.
Or maybe the handle needs to be moved to a position that aligns with the purple dots. The purple dots are at rows 51-58, cols 12-19. The handle is at rows 17-22, cols 12-42. They don't overlap, so this doesn't work.
I'm stuck. Let me try clicking on the green bar to see if it changes the paint color.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Handle moved DOWN, orientation changed to vertical. Paint still green.
# Let me try clicking on the green bar on the right edge to see if it changes paint color.
# The green bar is on the right edge, around col 62-63.

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 62}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
